plainMeaning: Each of these six kinds of underworld god-realms holds seventy-six lakh Jina-temples, a vastness that gladdens every soul headed for liberation.
recitation: e chae sthānaka jāṇīe, pratyeke jinhara | chahuṁtera chahuṁtera lākha tihāṁ, bhaviaṇa jana sukhakara

wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati demonstrative e, from Sanskrit eta-/etad ('this'); here pointing back to the six god-types listed in the preceding verse.
wordByWord.notes: Anaphoric: it gathers up Dīpa-, Diśā-, Udadhi-, Vidyut-, Stanita- and Agni-kumāra named just before.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati cha ('six', from Sanskrit ṣaṭ) + emphatic -e ('all six together'); the -e marks totality, 'the whole set of six'.
Sanskrit sthānaka ('station, fixed place'), from √sthā ('to stand'); here each Bhavanapati class counted as one 'station'.
wordByWord.notes: Means a category/abode of the lower-world gods, not a temple itself.

wordByWord.gloss: let us know / one should know
wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati jāṇīe (hortative/passive of jāṇvuṁ 'to know'), from Prakrit jāṇai, Sanskrit √jñā ('to know').
wordByWord.notes: Didactic 'be it known' — addressed to the reciter.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit pratyeka ('each separately') + locative -e; prati- ('each') + eka ('one').
wordByWord.notes: Distributive: the count applies to every one of the six, not to all six combined.

wordByWord.etymology: Apabhraṁśa/Gujarati jinhar from Sanskrit jina-gṛha ('Jina-house'); gṛha > har by Prakrit softening.
wordByWord.notes: Glossary term (jina-caitya); here plural in sense.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati chahoter/chahuṁter ('76'), from Prakrit chahattari < Sanskrit ṣaṭsaptati.
Reduplication of the same numeral for distributive force.
wordByWord.notes: The doubling means 'seventy-six apiece' — each of the six stations, not 76 in total.
So each of the six = 76,00,000 Jina-temples.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati tihāṁ ('there'), from Prakrit tahiṁ, Sanskrit tatra.
Prakrit bhaviya(ṇa) from Sanskrit bhavya ('one fit to become [liberated]'), √bhū; -aṇa is the Prakrit plural/genitive ending.
wordByWord.notes: Technical term: a jīva possessing the capacity for mokṣa (opp. abhavya).
Forms the phrase bhaviaṇa jana, 'the company of liberation-bound souls'.
Predicated of the temples/their contemplation: they gladden the bhavya.

literalTranslation: Know these six stations: in each, Jina-temples — seventy-six, seventy-six lakh there — joy-giving to the liberation-bound folk.
idiomaticTranslation: Know well these six classes of abode: in every single one of them stand Jina-temples — seventy-six lakh apiece — and there they fill with gladness every soul that is bound for liberation.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This verse is the arithmetic clasp that closes the catalogue of Bhavanapati gods opened in the previous line. Having just named Dīpa-, Diśā-, Udadhi-, Vidyut-, Stanita- and Agni-kumāra, the poet now gathers them with 'e chae sthānaka' ('these six stations') and assigns each a single shared figure. The force of the verse lies in two small words. 'Pratyeke' ('in each one') makes the count distributive, not cumulative; and the deliberate doubling 'chahuṁtera chahuṁtera' ('seventy-six, seventy-six') drives the same point home aurally — seventy-six lakh temples apiece, six times over, not seventy-six lakh shared among them. Six times seventy-six lakh yields four crore fifty-six lakh shrines from this group alone, a sub-total the very next verse will fold into the running census ('all gathered, seventy-two lakh more there, I bow to seven crore Jina-temples'). The closing epithet redirects the staggering number away from accountancy toward devotion: these unseen subterranean temples are 'bhaviaṇa jana sukhakara', a delight to souls capable of liberation. The point is not that a worshipper could ever visit them, but that merely knowing ('jāṇīe') of their boundless multitude lifts the bhavya heart. Even the lower-world gods, lowest of the four classes of deva, crown their mansions with houses of the Jina — so reverence saturates the cosmos from its very floor.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on two doctrines. First, the bhavya/abhavya distinction: the temples gladden specifically the 'bhaviaṇa jana', because only a soul with the innate capacity for mokṣa is moved by Jina-reverence; the figure is thus a test and tonic of one's own samyaktva. Second, Jaina cosmography places the Bhavanapati abodes in Ratnaprabhā, the topmost layer of the adho-loka, and holds their Jina-temples to be śāśvata — eternal, uncreated, ever-worshipped by the resident gods. That even the 'fiendish-youth' class of deity (asurakumāra) and its kin maintain crores of shrines establishes the verse's quiet claim: Jina-devotion is not a human institution but a structural feature of the universe itself.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recite the doubled 'chahuṁtera chahuṁtera' as a meditation on scale: let the number defeat the calculating mind and leave only awe. The reciter is not asked to verify the count but to let knowing it ('jāṇīe') soften pride and steady faith — joining, in spirit, the bhavya souls the verse blesses, and bowing inwardly to temples no pilgrim's feet will ever reach.

crossReferences.text: Tattvārtha-sūtra 4.10 (the ten classes of bhavanavāsin devas)
Saṃgrahaṇī-ratna (Śvetāmbara cosmography of god-realms and their caityas)

sources.title: Bhavanapati — definitions (Jaina Iconography; Triṣaṣṭiśalākāpuruṣacaritra)
sources.consultedFor: The ten Bhavanapati classes and confirmation that the six named (Dvīpa, Udadhi, Stanita, Vidyut, Dik/Diśā, Agni-kumāra) belong to this lower-world (adho-loka) group situated in Ratnaprabhā.

sources.title: Asurakumara — definitions (Tattvārtha-sūtra 4.10)
sources.consultedFor: Doctrinal placement of the bhavanavāsin gods as 'kumāra' (princely) dwellers in bhavanas of the uppermost hell, and their cosmographic sources (Saṃgrahaṇīratna / Trilokasāra).

sources.title: Bhavya — definitions
sources.consultedFor: Glossing 'bhaviaṇa' as bhavya — a jīva possessing the capacity for liberation, opposed to abhavya.

sources.title: Sthanaka — definitions
sources.consultedFor: Confirming 'sthānaka' as a fixed station/abode (√sthā), supporting its reading here as a class of god-realm rather than a temple.

notes: The '*' before ભવિઅણ is the book's page-bottom footnote marker (a pāṭhāntara/clarification), not part of the recited text, and is omitted from the recitation. The doubling 'chahuṁtera chahuṁtera' is distributive ('76 lakh in each of the six'), reinforced by 'pratyeke'; 6 × 76 lakh = 4,56,00,000, a sub-total the following verse rolls into the grand total of 7 crore 72 lakh Jina-temples for the whole Bhavanapati realm — the traditional figure (Asura 64 + Nāga 84 + Suparṇa 72 + the six at 76 each + Vāyu 96 lakh = 772 lakh). Numeral spellings (છહુંતેર, બહુંતેર) are kept verbatim per the book's orthography. The cosmographic counts are traditional devotional figures, preserved as printed rather than independently audited.
